# AI Description

# Technical Description of Figure A-4.2.2.3.-B

## 1. Diagram Type
This is a technical architectural detail drawing consisting of two views: a section view (top) and a plan view (bottom), illustrating daylighting zone calculations for sidelighted areas in building spaces.

## 2. Building Components and Systems Shown
- Vertical glazing (windows) in exterior walls
- Interior floor areas designated as primary and secondary sidelighted zones
- Vertical obstructions (walls or partitions) at 1.5 m or higher
- Ceiling and floor planes
- Opaque breaks between windows

## 3. Visible Labels, Measurements, and Annotations

### Section View (Top):
- **"top of glazing"** - marked at the uppermost point of the window
- **"head height (HH)"** - the vertical dimension from floor to top of glazing (shown as shaded area)
- **"Obstruction is 1.5 m or higher"** - notation indicating qualifying vertical obstructions on both left and right sides
- **"primary sidelighted area (1 × HH)"** - floor zone extending one head height from the glazing
- **"secondary sidelighted area (1 × HH)"** - floor zone extending one head height beyond the primary area

### Plan View (Bottom):
- **"½ × HH"** - marked at both top and bottom, indicating the lateral extension of sidelighted areas (half the head height on each side of window)
- **"Opaque break between windows is less than 1 × HH wide"** - notation on left side indicating the spacing condition between adjacent windows
- **"primary sidelighted area"** - the zone immediately adjacent to the glazing
- **"secondary sidelighted area"** - the zone beyond the primary area
- **"Obstruction is 1.5 m or higher"** - curved arrow pointing to vertical obstruction on right side

## 4. Relationships and Connections Between Elements

### Section View Relationships:
- The head height (HH) is the fundamental measurement unit, measured from floor to top of glazing
- The primary sidelighted area extends horizontally from the glazing a distance equal to 1 × HH
- The secondary sidelighted area begins at the edge of the primary area and extends an additional 1 × HH
- Vertical obstructions of 1.5 m or higher act as boundaries that terminate both primary and secondary sidelighted areas
- Both sidelighted zones are shown with equal depth (1 × HH each)

### Plan View Relationships:
- The width of sidelighted areas extends ½ × HH on each side of the window (both top and bottom in plan)
- When opaque breaks between windows are less than 1 × HH wide, the sidelighted areas from adjacent windows can overlap or merge
- The primary sidelighted area is shown immediately adjacent to the window location
- The secondary sidelighted area is positioned beyond the primary area
- Vertical obstructions of 1.5 m or higher limit the lateral spread of sidelighted areas
- The diagram demonstrates how sidelighted zones are bounded by both dimensional limits (½ × HH) and physical obstructions

## 5. Compliance Requirements and Technical Specifications

### Dimensional Requirements:
- **Head Height (HH)**: The critical reference dimension measured from floor to top of glazing
- **Primary Sidelighted Area Depth**: Extends 1 × HH perpendicular to the glazing
- **Secondary Sidelighted Area Depth**: Extends 1 × HH beyond the primary area
- **Lateral Extension**: Sidelighted areas extend ½ × HH on each side of the window width

### Obstruction Criteria:
- Vertical obstructions must be **1.5 m or higher** to qualify as boundaries for sidelighted areas
- Obstructions terminate the extension of both primary and secondary sidelighted zones

### Window Spacing:
- Opaque breaks between windows less than 1 × HH wide allow for potential overlap of sidelighted areas
- This condition affects the calculation of total sidelighted areas (avoiding double-counting as referenced in the code)

### Area Calculation Method:
- Primary sidelighted area = width (window width + ½ HH on each side, limited by obstructions) × depth (1 × HH, limited by obstructions)
- Secondary sidelighted area = width
